data_sources.market_config: 8 named competitors with positioning, pricing, and weakness data

market_sophistication.stage_name: Mechanism Fatigue — Transition to Enhanced/Unique Mechanism Required
market_sophistication.diagnosis_summary: The premium putter market ($300-$550) has fully entered Stage 4. LAB Golf's zero-torque mechanism (Stage 3 success story) was so effective that 4 major OEMs copied it within 18 months. The original mechanism is now commoditized. Simple mechanism claims ('zero torque,' 'high MOI,' 'AI face') no longer differentiate. The market demands ENHANCED mechanisms (genuinely new physics, not incremental improvements) or begins transitioning to Stage 5 identity/belief positioning. RS1 enters at the exact inflection point where a new, genuinely differentiated mechanism can capture the market before full Stage 5 identity fatigue sets in.

evidence.indicator: Zero torque mechanism copied by 4 major OEMs within 18 months
evidence.source: competitor_analysis.json — TaylorMade Spider ZT, Odyssey S2S, Bettinardi Antidote, Evnroll Z1 all launched ZT variants
evidence.significance: The defining Stage 3→4 transition signal: when competitors copy the breakout mechanism, the mechanism loses differentiation power. Zero torque went from category-defining to table stakes.

evidence.indicator: LAB Golf forum quote: 'Because LAB's whole thing is ZERO torque, they've kind of designed themselves into a corner'
evidence.significance: Market participants are identifying the mechanism's LIMITATIONS, not celebrating its innovation. This is textbook Stage 4 fatigue — the audience has internalized the mechanism and moved to evaluating its weaknesses.

evidence.indicator: High MOI / Forgiveness rated 'terminal saturation' — every major brand claims it
evidence.significance: The original Stage 3 mechanism (high MOI) has completed its full lifecycle from innovation to commodity. Zero differentiation remains. Any brand claiming 'forgiveness' is speaking a dead language.

evidence.indicator: Tour-proven / tour-winning claims used by 3+ brands simultaneously
evidence.source: competitor_analysis.json — TaylorMade, Scotty Cameron, Odyssey all claim tour validation
evidence.significance: Stage 2 enlarged claims ('most tour wins') have been exhausted by simultaneous usage. The claim has been diluted to meaninglessness by shared ownership.

evidence.indicator: CNC milled precision claimed by 5+ brands as differentiator
evidence.source: mechanism_inventory.json — Scotty Cameron, Bettinardi, Evnroll, Ping PLD, PXG
evidence.significance: Manufacturing process claims (Stage 3 credibility mechanisms) are now table stakes. Saying 'precision milled' differentiates no one.

evidence.indicator: AI-designed face (Odyssey Ai-ONE) already generating eye-rolls — rated 5/10 mechanism strength and declining
evidence.significance: Even a NOVEL mechanism claim (AI-designed) is failing because the market is sophisticated enough to detect marketing wrapped in technology language. Stage 4 audiences can distinguish genuine innovation from repackaged claims.

evidence.indicator: Golfer skepticism: 'The data all shows that tech in this particular tool doesn't matter much'
evidence.significance: A segment of the market has reached mechanism cynicism — they've heard so many mechanism claims that they've concluded none matter. This is the Stage 4 danger zone where audiences stop believing mechanisms altogether.

evidence.indicator: Forum debates about whether ANY putter technology actually matters
evidence.source: mechanism_inventory.json — implied mechanism 'confidence is the real mechanism' (54 quotes)
evidence.significance: When the audience starts arguing that confidence trumps technology, they've absorbed and exhausted multiple mechanism generations. Only a genuinely NEW mechanism with visible proof can break through this fatigue.

evidence.indicator: Bettinardi positioning Antidote as 'zero torque that looks good' — mechanism enhancement through aesthetics
evidence.significance: Classic Stage 4 move: when the mechanism itself is commoditized, brands try to differentiate by ENHANCING the mechanism with secondary benefits (better looks, better feel, better price). This confirms the base mechanism is no longer sufficient alone.

evidence.indicator: Odyssey S2S differentiating through shaft angle (not offset grip) for same zero-torque result
evidence.significance: Another Stage 4 enhancement: same mechanism, different execution method. When brands compete on HOW they deliver the mechanism rather than WHETHER the mechanism works, the mechanism itself is commoditized.

evidence.indicator: Forward CG acknowledged as 'most technically advanced CG location' by enthusiasts but unclaimed by major brands
evidence.significance: The market's most sophisticated participants are already looking BEYOND zero torque for the next mechanism. They've identified forward CG as superior but no major brand has claimed it. This is the Stage 4 opportunity window.

evidence.indicator: LAB Golf's tribal community and identity-based following ('I'm a LAB person')
evidence.source: competitor_analysis.json — LAB reception: 'can't go back' loyalty, community attachment
evidence.significance: LAB has Stage 5 elements: identity attachment, tribal community, belief system ('zero torque is the way'). But this is limited to one brand, not the full market. The broader market is still in Stage 4.

evidence.indicator: Scotty Cameron buyers purchase for status identity, not technology claims
evidence.source: competitor_analysis.json — 'The Rolex of putters, status symbol that also performs'
evidence.significance: Another Stage 5 signal: buying based on identity/worldview ('I'm a Scotty person') rather than mechanism evaluation. But this is heritage-driven identity, not mechanism-evolution-driven identity.

common_claims.effectiveness: Dead — zero differentiation. Every mallet claims high MOI. The term 'forgiving' has been stripped of meaning through overuse. Audience mentally filters this claim.
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Completed Stage 3 lifecycle. Now background noise.

common_claims.effectiveness: Declining — was the breakthrough Stage 3 mechanism when LAB owned it exclusively. Now commoditized by 4 copycats. Still works for LAB due to first-mover brand association but rapidly losing differentiation power for followers.
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Peak Stage 3 mechanism transitioning to Stage 4 commodity.

common_claims.effectiveness: Weak — three brands simultaneously claiming tour dominance cancels out the claim. Audiences recognize tour usage is influenced by endorsement deals. Still carries credibility weight but doesn't differentiate.
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Stage 2 enlarged claim, exhausted by shared ownership.

common_claims.effectiveness: Table stakes — CNC milling is standard manufacturing in premium putters. Claiming it is like claiming your car has an engine. Zero differentiation.
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Stage 3 credibility mechanism, now fully commoditized.

common_claims.effectiveness: Declining — initial novelty has worn off. Serious golfers perceive 'AI-designed' as a marketing buzzword, not genuine innovation. Rated 5/10 mechanism strength and falling.
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Attempted Stage 3 mechanism that failed to gain traction. Audience sophistication outpaced the claim.

common_claims.effectiveness: Subjective and non-differentiating — every premium putter claims great feel. 'Feel' is a personal preference, not a technology claim. Works for retention (current users love their putter's feel) but not acquisition (prospects can't evaluate feel from marketing).
common_claims.stage_lifecycle: Perennial Stage 1/2 claim that never reaches mechanism territory because it's inherently subjective.

exhausted_claims: High MOI / most forgiving — used by 6+ brands, zero differentiation
Tour-proven / most wins on tour — 3+ brands claim simultaneously, cancels out
Precision CNC milled — standard manufacturing, not a feature
Game-changing / revolutionary — emptied of meaning by a decade of overuse
Best alignment system — every putter has lines, dots, or rails
Most stable putter — stability claims are universal in mallets
Custom fitting available — every premium brand offers fitting
Premium materials / multi-material construction — standard in $300+ putters
AI/algorithm designed face — perceived as marketing, not innovation
Consistent ball speed across the face — only Evnroll has proven this, but the claim itself is becoming generic
still_effective_claims: Named root cause + named solution ('face drift' + 'forward weighting') — ONLY works when the claim introduces NEW vocabulary the market hasn't heard. RS1 owns this.
Visual proof demonstration (face-down balance test) — physical demonstrations cut through Stage 4 skepticism because they bypass verbal claims entirely
Named designer with verifiable OEM credentials — Chris McGinley's 25 years + 11 world #1 players is a credibility claim no competitor can match because no one else has it
Active vs passive distinction — reframing an existing category (zero torque) with a new dimension (active/passive) creates fresh evaluation criteria
No adjustment period required — addresses the #1 adoption barrier across all ZT putters, and no competitor can credibly make this claim
Price advantage against the category leader — $399/$429 vs LAB $449-$649 while claiming technological superiority
Steel face feel in a face-stable mallet — addresses LAB's #1 material complaint (aluminum face) with a specific, tangible counter

saturated_mechanisms.lifecycle_position: Post-Stage 3 — fully commoditized. Every premium mallet claims high MOI. No audience attention remains for this claim.
saturated_mechanisms.rs1_implication: NEVER lead with MOI or forgiveness. RS1 achieves high MOI through forward weighting but this should be a footnote, not a headline.

saturated_mechanisms.lifecycle_position: Late Stage 3 → Early Stage 4. Was a breakthrough mechanism 2-3 years ago (LAB exclusive). Now offered by 5 brands. Within 12-18 months, ZT will be as saturated as high MOI.
saturated_mechanisms.rs1_implication: DO NOT position RS1 as zero torque. This would make RS1 another me-too entry in an increasingly crowded field. Position as BEYOND zero torque — the next evolution in face control.

saturated_mechanisms.lifecycle_position: Fully commoditized manufacturing claim. Standard process, not a differentiator.
saturated_mechanisms.rs1_implication: Mention CNC milling for credibility (material quality) but never as a differentiating feature.
RS1's Face Square Focus (triple parallel lines) is good design but NOT a headline differentiator.

emerging_mechanisms.mechanism: Zero Torque by Major OEMs (second-generation ZT)
emerging_mechanisms.growth_indicator: Rapid — 4 new ZT entrants in 18 months. Validates the face-squaring category but commoditizes the original mechanism.
emerging_mechanisms.trend_direction: Accelerating toward saturation. Within 12-18 months, 'zero torque' will be the new 'high MOI' — claimed by everyone, believed by no one as differentiation.
emerging_mechanisms.rs1_implication: The ZT wave HELPS RS1 by educating the market on face-squaring importance. RS1 can draft behind this education and say 'Now here's the BETTER way to achieve it.' But RS1 must never be lumped in with the ZT crowd.

emerging_mechanisms.growth_indicator: Growing — brands are differentiating their ZT entries with secondary benefits
emerging_mechanisms.trend_direction: Classic Stage 4 enhancement cycle. The base mechanism is commoditized so brands add layers.
emerging_mechanisms.rs1_implication: RS1 is NOT an enhanced ZT product. It uses a fundamentally different physics principle (forward CG creating active face stabilization vs ZT's passive torque elimination). This distinction is critical for positioning.

competitors_list: Axis1 (niche brand with minimal market presence)
emerging_mechanisms.growth_indicator: Nascent — acknowledged by enthusiasts as 'most technically advanced CG location' but unclaimed by any major brand
emerging_mechanisms.trend_direction: Pre-breakout. The concept exists in technical discussions but has no mainstream brand champion.
emerging_mechanisms.rs1_implication: THIS IS RS1's MECHANISM. First-mover advantage is enormous. RS1 can define and own this entire category the way LAB defined and owned zero torque.

emerging_mechanisms.growth_indicator: Stable — independently validated but not widely copied. Likely to remain Evnroll's proprietary niche.
emerging_mechanisms.trend_direction: Plateau. Other brands haven't attempted to copy it, suggesting it may remain a single-brand mechanism.
emerging_mechanisms.rs1_implication: SweetFace solves a DIFFERENT problem (mishit distance control) than RS1 (face squaring). Not competitive — potentially complementary. RS1 messaging should acknowledge Evnroll's face tech excellence while positioning RS1 as solving the more fundamental problem.

opportunity_mechanisms: Forward Weighting / Heavy Forward CG — 75% of head weight in front 25% of head. NO major brand claims this. RS1's hero mechanism.
Face Down Balance (FDB) — the putter naturally falls face-down when released. ZERO competitors demonstrate this property. RS1's visual proof mechanism.
Active Face Control — face stabilization through gravity and forward CG (active opposing force) vs zero torque (passive twist removal). RS1's differentiating frame.
Face Drift as Named Root Cause — the consumer-friendly term for why putts miss. NO competitor has named this root cause. LAB names 'torque.' RS1 names 'face drift.'
Technology + Technique dual promise — the putter improves both the ball and the player's stroke. ZERO competitors make this claim.
74-Degree Square-to-Square Lie Angle as designed-in performance feature — competitors treat lie angle as a fitting variable. RS1 designed it in as a feature.
No-Adjustment Face-Squaring — works with existing stroke from day one. Every ZT competitor requires 2-8 weeks adaptation.
Gravity-Driven Pendulum — forward weighting creates natural pendulum motion that improves distance control intuitively
Designer-Led Innovation Authority — Chris McGinley's 25-year OEM pedigree as the credibility mechanism itself

positioning_patterns.effectiveness: Declining — was highly effective when LAB owned it exclusively. Now diluted by 4 followers. The mechanism is understood and valued by the market, but the competitive advantage belongs to no single brand (except LAB through first-mover association). Followers are fighting for scraps of an increasingly commoditized positioning.
positioning_patterns.vulnerability: Passive ZT removes twist but doesn't actively HELP the player square the face. Requires adaptation period. Creates distance control complaints. These are structural weaknesses of the physics, not execution failures — they apply to ALL ZT putters.

positioning_patterns.effectiveness: Stable but narrowing — appeals to identity/status buyers (Stage 5 adjacent) but offers no mechanism-level differentiation. Scotty Cameron retains premium brand power but is losing technology-forward buyers to LAB and ZT alternatives. Bettinardi is respected but invisible. Ping is reliable but unexciting.
positioning_patterns.vulnerability: No proprietary face-control technology. Performance advantages over $200 alternatives are marginal. Technology-forward buyers are migrating to mechanism-based brands.

positioning_patterns.effectiveness: Exhausted — high MOI is table stakes. Brand trust maintains sales volume but creates no differentiation. These brands are playing a volume game, not an innovation game.
positioning_patterns.vulnerability: MOI addresses SYMPTOMS (forgiveness on mishits) not CAUSES (why the face drifts). Technology-conscious buyers see through this as a half-measure.

positioning_patterns.pattern: Mishit Correction Technology (Face Grooves / AI Insert)
positioning_patterns.effectiveness: Moderate for Evnroll (independently validated), weak for Odyssey (perceived as marketing). Both address ball-speed consistency on mishits — a real problem but not the PRIMARY problem for most frustrated putters.
positioning_patterns.vulnerability: Addresses what happens AFTER the mishit, not what CAUSES the mishit. Doesn't compete in the face-squaring conversation. Secondary problem space.

positioning_patterns.effectiveness: Niche — attracts deal-seekers and value-conscious buyers. Effective within its segment but doesn't drive the innovation conversation.
positioning_patterns.vulnerability: Frequent sales and aggressive pricing undermine premium perception. No unique technology story.

crowded_positions: Zero Torque / Face-Squaring (Passive) — 5 brands competing for the same positioning. Rapidly approaching terminal saturation.
High MOI / Forgiveness — 6+ brands claiming variations of the same thing. Already at terminal saturation.
Tour-Proven / Most Wins — 3 brands fighting for the same claim with diminishing returns.
Precision Craftsmanship — 5 brands claiming CNC milling excellence. No differentiation.
Better-Looking Zero Torque — Bettinardi Antidote occupies this niche, but it's inherently limited (still passive ZT).
white_space: Active Face-Squaring Technology — ZERO competitors occupy this space. RS1's Face Down Balance is the only active face-stabilization mechanism in the market. Every ZT putter is passive.
Named Root Cause Ownership ('Face Drift') — NO competitor has named and owned WHY putts miss in consumer-friendly language. LAB calls it 'torque' (technical). RS1 can own 'face drift' (experiential).

Technology + Technique Dual Promise — ZERO competitors claim their putter improves the player's stroke mechanics. All claim compensation/forgiveness. RS1 uniquely claims both technology AND technique improvement.
Forward Weighting Category — Only Axis1 (niche, minimal presence) uses forward CG. No major brand has claimed this territory. RS1 can be the first major brand to own forward weighting.
No-Adjustment Face Squaring — Every ZT putter requires 2-8 weeks adaptation. RS1 claims to work with existing stroke from day one. This is unclaimed territory.
Mallet Stability + Blade Feel — Many golfers want both but no major brand explicitly promises this combination. RS1's steel face in a forward-weighted mallet body delivers both.

Designer Authority (Named OEM Veteran) — No competitor has a named designer with 25-year OEM pedigree and 11 world #1 players as an active brand ambassador. Scotty Cameron is a brand, not a person sharing technical insights.

stage_1_signals.signal: Simple direct claims work ('This putter works')
stage_1_signals.evidence: No premium putter brand succeeds with simple direct claims. The market is far too sophisticated for 'buy this putter and putt better.' Even the least sophisticated buyers in the corpus (Reddit r/golf casual golfers) discuss technical specifications.

stage_1_signals.signal: No established mechanism language in market
stage_1_signals.evidence: The market has extensive mechanism vocabulary: zero torque, MOI, face balanced, toe hang, lie angle balance, SweetFace, AI-designed. Technical language is deeply embedded in consumer discussions.

stage_2_signals.evidence: 'Most tour wins' is claimed by 3 brands simultaneously, canceling out. 'Best feel' is subjective and undifferentiating. 'Most forgiving' is claimed by 6+ brands. Enlarged claims have been exhausted.

stage_2_signals.signal: Brand names and endorsements drive purchase
stage_2_signals.evidence: Scotty Cameron still sells primarily on brand name and tour endorsements — but this is narrowing. Brand-driven buyers are a SHRINKING segment as technology-forward buyers migrate to mechanism-based brands (LAB, Evnroll). Stage 2 signals are present but receding.
stage_2_signals.note: Residual Stage 2 behavior exists in the Scotty Cameron buyer segment but does not represent the market's direction.

stage_3_signals.evidence: LAB Golf's entire success story is a Stage 3 mechanism triumph. Zero torque as a physics-based mechanism created a new category and drove massive growth. The market rewards mechanism stories — LAB went from niche to $200M acquisition on mechanism strength alone.

stage_3_signals.signal: Consumers discuss HOW products work, not just WHETHER they work
stage_3_signals.evidence: GolfWRX and Reddit discussions extensively analyze zero torque physics, CG placement, MOI calculations, face angle dynamics. 38% of the addressable market is Solution-Aware, meaning they evaluate mechanism categories, not just products.

stage_3_signals.signal: Independent testing and data validate mechanism claims
stage_3_signals.evidence: MyGolfSpy testing is a market-moving force. Evnroll's SweetFace won multiple Most Wanted awards on independent data. Arccos strokes-gained data is cited in purchase decisions. The market values mechanism validation.

stage_3_signals.signal: New mechanism stories still have power
stage_3_signals.evidence: A genuinely NEW mechanism (not a copy) can still break through. Forward CG is acknowledged by enthusiasts as 'the most technically advanced CG location' (Q0463). The appetite for mechanism innovation is intact — what's exhausted is RECYCLED mechanism claims.
stage_3_signals.note: This is the critical Stage 3/4 distinction: Stage 3 mechanism appetite remains, but only for genuinely NEW mechanisms. Copies and enhancements of existing mechanisms are Stage 4 moves that yield diminishing returns.

stage_4_signals.signal: The breakout mechanism (zero torque) has been copied by multiple competitors
stage_4_signals.evidence: 5 brands now offer zero-torque putters: LAB Golf, TaylorMade Spider ZT, Odyssey S2S, Bettinardi Antidote, Evnroll Z1. The mechanism that defined Stage 3 is now commoditized.

stage_4_signals.signal: Brands differentiate through mechanism ENHANCEMENTS rather than new mechanisms
stage_4_signals.evidence: Bettinardi: 'ZT that looks good.' Odyssey S2S: 'ZT via shaft angle.' Evnroll Z1: 'ZT + SweetFace grooves.' Every new ZT entry adds a secondary benefit to the same base mechanism — classic Stage 4 enhancement behavior.

Q0385: 'The data all shows that tech in this particular tool doesn't matter much.' Q0375: 'There is almost zero data to support the categorization of stroke types and the putters that work best for them.' A significant segment is questioning whether ANY mechanism claims are real.

stage_4_signals.signal: Price becomes a louder part of the value equation
stage_4_signals.evidence: Q0471: 'the price for a counterbalance is hefty.' Q0384: '$500+ is a lot of money to throw at a hope.' Q0812: LAB knockoff on TEMU for $90. When mechanisms commoditize, price sensitivity increases because buyers see less differentiation to justify premium pricing.

stage_4_signals.signal: Need for genuinely UNIQUE mechanism to break through
stage_4_signals.evidence: Axis1's forward CG praised as 'most technically advanced CG location' despite minimal marketing. Enthusiasts are actively seeking the next innovation beyond zero torque. The market WANTS a Stage 4 breakthrough mechanism — forward weighting is that mechanism.

stage_4_signals.signal: Mechanism claims must be PROVED, not just stated
stage_4_signals.evidence: LAB's Revealer tool, MyGolfSpy independent testing, Arccos data, SAM PuttLab measurements — the market demands tangible proof. Verbal mechanism claims without demonstration are dismissed. RS1's face-down balance test is a Stage 4-appropriate proof mechanism.

stage_5_signals.evidence: LAB Golf has a tribal following: 'can't go back,' 'I'm a LAB person,' community identification. Scotty Cameron has collector/status identity. These are genuine Stage 5 behaviors — but limited to specific brand ecosystems, not the full market.
stage_5_signals.note: Stage 5 elements exist within LAB Golf and Scotty Cameron buyer segments but the MARKET as a whole has not reached Stage 5. The broader market is still mechanism-driven (Stage 3/4).

stage_5_signals.signal: Purchase driven by worldview/belief rather than feature comparison
stage_5_signals.evidence: LAB converts express belief-level commitment: 'the science is real,' 'I believe in the LAB approach.' Scotty buyers express identity-level attachment: 'I'm a Scotty person.' But the MAJORITY of the market still compares features and mechanisms — belief-driven purchasing is a segment behavior, not market-wide.
stage_5_signals.note: Approximately 15-20% of the premium putter market exhibits Stage 5 behavior (LAB evangelists + Scotty loyalists). The remaining 80% is in Stage 3/4 mechanism evaluation mode.

stage_5_signals.evidence: Market config defines anti-identity: 'The guy who buys the latest shiny putter every year and still can't putt. The sucker who falls for marketing.' Golfers explicitly don't want to be this person. This anti-identity consciousness is a Stage 5 signal.
stage_5_signals.note: RS1 can layer Stage 5 identity elements ('For golfers who think differently about putting') on top of a Stage 4 breakthrough mechanism. The identity layer enhances the mechanism story but should not REPLACE it.

strategic_implications.messaging_strategy: Lead with a genuinely NEW Stage 4 mechanism (Forward Weighting / Face Down Balance) that the market has never seen, supported by tangible proof (the face-down balance demonstration). Layer Stage 5 identity elements as a secondary dimension. The message architecture should be: (1) Name the enemy — 'face drift' (new vocabulary that reframes the problem), (2) Reveal the mechanism — forward weighting moves CG in FRONT of the face, (3) Prove it — the face-down balance test anyone can do, (4) Differentiate from the known — 'zero torque prevents twist; forward weighting actively keeps the face square,' (5) Layer identity — 'for golfers who trust physics over marketing.' This architecture works because it introduces new information at every step rather than recycling exhausted claims.
strategic_implications.claim_approach: Stage 4 demands UNIQUE claims backed by tangible proof. Generic claims ('most forgiving,' 'tour-proven,' 'precision milled') will be ignored. RS1's claims must pass the 'has anyone said this before?' test. Forward weighting: NO — unique to RS1. Face-down balance: NO — unique to RS1. Face drift as root cause: NO — RS1 coined it. Active vs passive face control: NO — new category distinction. Technology + technique dual promise: NO — no competitor claims both. Every RS1 headline claim must be defensibly unique. Supporting claims (steel face, premium construction, competitive price) provide credibility but don't lead.
strategic_implications.mechanism_approach: Roll Straight Technology should be presented as a SYSTEM (7 engineered features working together) rather than a single technology claim. This 'integrated system' positioning is a Stage 4 power move because it's harder to copy than a single feature and creates a more compelling story than any individual component. The hierarchy: (1) Heavy Forward Weighting (the hero — 75% of weight in front 25% of head), (2) Face Down Balance (the visual proof — the putter falls face-down), (3) 74-Degree Square-to-Square Lie Angle (the stroke path), supported by Dual Pistol Grip, T-Wing Head Design, 360g Head Weight, and CNC Machined Steel Face. Lead with #1, prove with #2, reinforce with #3.
strategic_implications.differentiation_path: RS1's differentiation path is the Evolutionary Narrative positioned at the Stage 3→4 mechanism transition point. The story: Traditional putters had a design flaw (face drift — CG behind the shaft). LAB Golf identified it and solved HALF the problem with zero torque (passive twist removal). RS1 solves the FULL problem with Forward Weighting (active face stabilization). This narrative respects LAB's innovation, educates the audience on why ZT is incomplete, and positions RS1 as the next chapter — not a competitor, but an evolution. The evolutionary frame also future-proofs RS1 against commoditization because it positions forward weighting as the DESTINATION, not a stop along the way.

recommended_positioning.primary_strategy: Enter at Stage 4 with a genuinely new mechanism (Forward Weighting / Face Down Balance) that occupies unclaimed white space, while layering Stage 5 identity elements. RS1 should NOT position as zero torque (Stage 3 commodity), NOT position on MOI/forgiveness (terminal saturation), and NOT position on tour validation (unavailable at launch). Instead, position as THE EVOLUTION BEYOND ZERO TORQUE — the first putter to use active face control through forward weighting, demonstrated by a visual proof (face-down balance) that no competitor can replicate. The positioning statement: 'Zero torque removed the twist. Forward weighting controls the face. RS1 is the first putter engineered to actively keep your face square — not by removing a problem, but by creating a solution.'
supporting_tactics: Name and own the root cause — 'Face drift' as the enemy. No competitor has named why putts miss in consumer-friendly language. RS1 coins the term and owns the problem definition. Whoever names the problem owns the solution conversation.

Visual proof over verbal claims — The face-down balance test is RS1's Revealer. In a Stage 4 market drowning in mechanism claims, a physical demonstration that anyone can perform in 2 seconds cuts through all skepticism. Lead demos, videos, and retail experiences with this test.

Active vs. Passive frame — Create a new evaluation dimension. Don't compete within the zero-torque category (too crowded). Instead, create a NEW category distinction: passive face control (ZT — the putter doesn't hinder you) vs. active face control (FDB — the putter HELPS you). This forces every ZT putter into the 'passive' bucket and positions RS1 alone in the 'active' bucket.

Designer credibility — Chris McGinley (25 years OEM, 11 world #1 players) provides the authority a new mechanism claim requires. In Stage 4, audiences are skeptical of brand claims. An engineer with verifiable credentials is more credible than a brand story.

Price wedge against LAB — RS1 at $399/$429 vs LAB at $449-$649. Position as 'more technology for less money' but lead with the technology advantage, not the price advantage. Price validates; it doesn't differentiate.

Address every LAB blocker — Aesthetics (premium mallet vs UFO), feel (steel face vs aluminum), adaptation (works day one vs 2-8 weeks), distance control (natural pendulum vs counterbalanced), shaft position (heel-shaft option vs center-shaft only). RS1 is designed as the LAB blocker-breaker.

Evolutionary narrative — Frame the market's history: Old putters (face drifts) → LAB (passive fix) → RS1 (active fix). This positions RS1 as the culmination, not a competitor. Respectful of LAB's innovation, but clearly the next step.
avoid: NEVER position as zero torque or lie-angle balanced — this makes RS1 the 6th entry in a rapidly commoditizing category. RS1 is BEYOND zero torque.
NEVER lead with 'high MOI' or 'forgiving' — terminal saturation. Every mallet claims this. It's invisible to the audience.

NEVER claim 'tour-proven' or cite tour wins — RS1 has no tour validation at launch. Attempting this claim creates a credibility gap that undermines the real mechanism story. Use designer credentials instead.
NEVER use 'game-changing,' 'revolutionary,' or 'next-level' — these have been emptied of meaning by a decade of golf marketing overuse. Every sentence must be specific and provable.
NEVER use 'AI-designed' or algorithm language — the market has flagged this as marketing hype. RS1's mechanism is physics-based and demonstrable, not algorithmic.

NEVER attack LAB Golf adversarially — the evolutionary narrative requires respect for LAB's innovation. Attacking LAB creates defensiveness in the very audience RS1 needs to convert (LAB-curious and LAB-frustrated golfers).

NEVER fabricate performance data or testing results — Stage 4 audiences verify claims aggressively. Any hallucinated stat will be caught and will destroy credibility permanently. Every number must trace to Chris McGinley's product docs or verified testing.

NEVER lead with price — RS1's price advantage is real ($399 vs $449-$649 for LAB) but leading with price positions RS1 as a budget alternative rather than a technological advancement. Price validates the value proposition; the mechanism is the headline.

NEVER position as 'just another weighting scheme' — the biggest risk is that forward weighting gets dismissed as incremental rather than revolutionary. The face-down balance visual proof exists specifically to counter this: 'Hold any putter and let it fall — the face opens. Hold the RS1 — the face goes DOWN. That's not an adjustment. That's different physics.'
